channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Bridges and Water Plants Hit as Strikes Stretch to 7th Straight Day
item.description: Overnight and into Friday, Iran reported U.S. strikes on infrastructure that can serve civilian purposes and has targeted similar sites in Gulf countries that host American bases. In the afternoon, the U.S. military announced a new round of attacks on Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.media:credit: Emile Ducke for The New York Times

item.title: Trump’s Election Claims and SAVE Act Push Find Muted Response From G.O.P. Lawmakers
item.description: Most G.O.P. lawmakers had little to say about the president’s claims of election vulnerabilities, and he did not appear to move the needle on the voting restriction bill he championed.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold, Carl Hulse, Reid J. Epstein and Erik Wemple
item.media:description: President Trump, seen on a screen during his speech at the White House on Thursday. Mr. Trump has made baseless assertions of election fraud for years, largely pointing to a similar set of concerns.

item.title: Trump Pursues a Deeper Bond With China’s Leader, Despite Hostile Speech
item.description: Chinese officials appear to think that President Trump’s accusations that China interfered in the 2020 U.S. elections were driven by domestic politics, not foreign policy.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.media:description: Xi Jinping and President Trump participated in events at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing in May.

item.title: Bridges and Water Plants Attacked as Iran War Escalates
item.description: Also, U.S. start-up activity is booming. Here’s the latest at the end of Friday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:description: An image from social media shows bridges reduced to rubble in southern Iran.

item.title: Trump Faces the Limits of U.S. Firepower and the Lessons of Past Wars
item.description: Like his predecessors, President Trump has struggled to turn battlefield successes into long-term victories.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We’re going to knock out all their power plants,” President Trump told Fox News this week. “We’re going to knock out all their bridges unless they get to the table and negotiate.”

item.title: Oil Prices Rise to Highest Level in a Month as Shipping in the Persian Gulf Nears a Halt
item.description: Shipping traffic was at its lowest level in more than a month and oil prices moved higher as the Iran war continued to disrupt the supply of energy.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: China’s Moonshot AI Unveils Kimi Model, Threatening America’s Lead
item.description: China’s Moonshot AI unveiled a freely available artificial intelligence model that seemed to narrow the gap with cutting-edge offerings from U.S. tech companies.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin and Cade Metz
item.media:description: The Moonshot AI booth at the World Artificial Intelligence Conference on Friday in Shanghai. The company’s Kimi K3 model performed as well at some tasks as the leading models from U.S. firms.

item.title: Stocks Sink on Anxiety About Tech and A.I. Spending
item.description: Investors are growing uneasy about increasing competition from China in the global race to dominate artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison and Katie Robertson

item.title: Meta in Talks to Lease Computing Power to Anthropic in Potential $10 Billion Deal
item.description: A deal would underline how scarce computing power is for artificial intelligence development, and could create a new business for Meta.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Dustin Chambers for The New York Times
item.media:description: A data center owned by Meta in Georgia. A deal with Anthropic could potentially alleviate pressure on Meta from investors, who have questioned how much it has been spending on data centers.

item.title: Trump Threatens Canada With Tariffs for Wildfire Smoke Over U.S.
item.description: The president said on Friday in a social media post that Canada was failing to manage its wildfire response as smoke has blanketed U.S. cities including New York and Chicago.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen and Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: President Trump departing Joint Base Andrews, en route to New York, on Friday.

item.title: Dozens of Wildfires Are Suddenly Burning in Oregon
item.description: The state told thousands of people to be ready to evacuate.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff

item.title: When Will the Smoke End? Our Meteorologist Explains Why It’s Complicated.
item.description: The interplay between the burning fires, the smoke itself and quickly changing weather patterns is making the next few days more uncertain than usual.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Wildfire smoke hung over pickleball players in Manhattan on Thursday. While the smoke decreased in the city early Friday, it could return later as weather patterns change.

item.title: Watch the Wildfire Smoke Ooze Across North America From Space
item.description: Satellite imagery shows the wide footprint of the smoke across the continent.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones

item.title: Taking Out the Trash: These Old Ladies Are Cleaning Up Cape Cod
item.description: The Old Ladies Against Underwater Garbage estimate they’ve removed 6,000 pounds of refuse from ponds on Cape Cod. “There’s no better feeling,” one said.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley and Billy Hickey
item.media:credit: Billy Hickey for The New York Times

item.title: Taylor Farms to Remove Products After Lettuce Is Linked to Cyclospora Cases
item.description: The California company grows produce in more than 12 states, and in Mexico and Canada. Taco Bell and food distributor Sysco have pulled Taylor Farms lettuce from supply chains.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell, Christina Jewett and Kevin Draper
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention

item.title: Cyclospora Investigation Turns to Farms in Mexico
item.description: The F.D.A. is planning to inspect farms and a lettuce shredding facility to determine how lettuce supplied by the produce company Taylor Farms to Taco Bell became contaminated and where it was shipped.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Dani Blum
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Food and Drug Administration’s investigation is focusing on the source of the contamination by the cyclospora parasite.

item.title: Is It Safe to Eat Lettuce?
item.description: Federal health officials have linked a large cyclosporiasis outbreak to shredded lettuce served at Taco Bell. Is lettuce from grocery stores and restaurants OK?
item.dc:creator: Dani Blum and Roni Caryn Rabin

item.title: Mourning One Tragedy at Sea, a Family Is Devastated by Another
item.description: The Boisas went out on the San Francisco Bay to spread the ashes of one of their own. Then a wave toppled their boat.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla, Madaleine Rubin and Heather Knight
item.media:credit: Stephen Lam/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Alcatraz Island a day after a boat named Volare capsized in rough seas near the island.

item.title: Firms With Checkered Pasts Tied to Troubled Midtown Construction Project
item.description: Among those connected to companies that worked on 235 East 42nd Street were a man who once bribed city officials and a developer who gave a loan to Bernard Kerik.
item.dc:creator: Dionne Searcey, William K. Rashbaum, Stefanos Chen and Mihir Zaveri
item.media:description: Two columns inside a Midtown high-rise construction project buckled last week, sowing panic and causing evacuations around the East 42nd Street site.

item.title: Sara Rodriguez, Lieutenant Governor, Quits Wisconsin Governor’s Race
item.description: Some Democrats feared the decision by Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez, the establishment candidate, could improve the primary prospects for Francesca Hong, a democratic socialist, in a purple state.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman
item.media:credit: Jenn Ackerman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez’s withdrawal scrambled the race in Wisconsin.

item.title: Billionaires Prepare $87 Million Ad Campaign to Block California Wealth Tax
item.description: A coalition backed by the Google co-founder Sergey Brin and wealthy tech investors is trying to neutralize a proposed one-time tax on the state’s richest residents.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Laurel Rosenhall
item.category: Service Employees International Union-United Healthcare Workers West
item.media:description: A campaign event for a proposed billionaire tax in Los Angeles in February.

item.title: Trump Endorses Graham for Senate, Scrambling South Carolina Primary
item.description: Senator Darline Graham had been seen as a caretaker for her brother’s seat after his sudden death. Now she has the president’s nod in what was shaping up to be a crowded fight to succeed Lindsey Graham.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: Darline Graham, left, was sworn in on Tuesday at the Capitol.

item.title: Who Really Has the 2026 Midterms Cash Edge?
item.description: The fund-raising race is tighter than it seems.
item.dc:creator: Jess Bidgood
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Ken Paxton last month at the 2026 Republican Party of Texas convention in Houston. Mr. Paxton, the party’s nominee for Senate, has been drastically out-raised by his Democratic rival, James Talarico.

item.title: The World Cup, in Photos
item.description: See some of the best images from the world’s most popular sporting event.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: On Fire. Again.
item.description: There were orange skies in 2023, 2025 and now again in 2026. Over the last few years, one thing has become glaringly clear: The East Coast is totally unequipped for the next climate disaster, wildfires. The Opinion writer David Wallace-Wells explains that with every climate disaster, we’re not just adapting to a “new normal,” but bracing ourselves for what’s next.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells

item.title: How Trump Abused the Power to Declassify America’s Secrets
item.description: The president is manipulating the intelligence community into a tool of misinformation.
item.dc:creator: Massimo Calabresi
item.media:credit: Joseph Rushmore for The New York Times

item.title: 7 Americans Sent to Disputed Kenya Ebola Site After New Trump Travel Ban
item.description: The aid workers were on the frontline in the fight against the outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo and have shown no symptoms, their employer said.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A satellite image showing the field hospital at Laikipia Air Base in central Kenya, where America aid workers are being quarantined.
